result:
The subeditor accepted the structure and made two small mechanical fixes only: trimmed a repeated "actually" (verbal tic), and split the stacked three-clause Mia sentence so the subject sits closer to its verbs. No queries.

Final email, text only (nothing sent):

---

**To:** Nadia Brook <nadia@example.com>
**Cc:** Mia Lee <marketing@example.com>
**From:** Sam Tan <sam@example.com>
**Subject:** After the trade exchange

Nadia,

Thank you for putting on the trade exchange. The day gave us a clear sense of what the hotel desks want from us.

It also showed us our concierge flyer is wrong. It leads with horse riding, when what suits a family is the whole day: the animals, the alpaca walk, the river and the café, with riding for whoever wants it. We are rebuilding it into something a concierge can hand over.

Mia, cc'd, handled our registration with you and runs our marketing. She will look after this from here, so she is your contact for it. Once it is drafted we would be grateful for your eye on it, since you know what the desks use far better than we do.

Sam

---

Note on what changed from the earlier draft: I cut the closing "what we want is… reach for… live and bookable… tell us" paragraph. It was positioning aimed at Nadia's commercial goal, which the skill's like-human-do pass flags as an ask dressed as warmth. The email now demonstrates that Hillside Farm is fixing its product and defers to her expertise, rather than narrating why that serves her. Cc to Mia is appropriate: it makes the handoff real and gives Nadia a named owner. Say the word if you want it sent or changed.
